narrative_goal: How does JWST help us understand star formation and the early universe?

scenes.title: Introduction
scenes.purpose: Set context and introduce the topic
scenes.narration: The James Webb Space Telescope is the most powerful space telescope ever built, allowing us to peer through cosmic dust and detect light from the earliest galaxies.
scenes.visual_brief: Wide establishing shot of JWST in space at the L2 point. Cinematic imagery with text overlay.
scenes.on_screen_text: How does JWST help us understand star formation and the early universe?

scenes.title: Infrared Vision Reveals Hidden Stellar Nurseries
scenes.purpose: Present key finding: infrared observations of star-forming regions
scenes.narration: JWST's NIRCam and MIRI instruments see through dense dust clouds, revealing protostars in the Carina Nebula and the Pillars of Creation.
scenes.visual_brief: Side-by-side comparison of visible vs infrared views of the Carina Nebula.
scenes.on_screen_text: Infrared Vision Reveals Hidden Stellar Nurseries

scenes.title: Earliest Galaxies Detected
scenes.purpose: Present key finding: discovery of early galaxies
scenes.narration: JWST has detected galaxies forming just 300-400 million years after the Big Bang, challenging existing models of galaxy formation.
scenes.visual_brief: Deep field image with highlighted early galaxy candidates and redshift labels.

scenes.title: Conclusion
scenes.purpose: Wrap up and point to future directions
scenes.narration: Looking ahead, JWST continues to transform our understanding of stellar nurseries and the dawn of the cosmos.
scenes.visual_brief: Cinematic pull-back shot from JWST. Summary text overlay with credits.
